claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

The first thing I need to know is about the child (or children) you're claiming for.

**How old is the child you're claiming Child Benefit for?**

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children you'd like to check eligibility for, please let me know their ages as well, and we can assess each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I've got one child - Alex is 17 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thanks for letting me know about Alex! Since Alex is 17 years old, they fall into the **16 to 19 age category**. For this age group, there are some additional requirements we need to check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For young people aged 16-19 like Alex, Child Benefit eligibility depends on their current situation with education or training.

**Which of the following best describes Alex's situation?**

1. **In education or training** - Alex is currently enrolled in approved education or training (such as A-levels, T-levels, NVQs, or similar qualifications)

2. **Recently left education** - Alex has recently finished or left education/training (within the last few months)

3. **Neither** - Alex is not in education or training and hasn't recently left

Which applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is currently in education - he's studying for his A-levels at sixth form.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great news! Alex is studying for A-levels at sixth form, which sounds like it should qualify. Let me just verify that this meets the approved education criteria.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
A-levels at sixth form is on the list of approved qualifications. I just need to confirm a few details to make sure it meets all the criteria:

**A-levels are an approved qualification** ✓

Now I need to check a couple more things:

1. **Is Alex studying full-time?** (This means at least 12 hours per week of supervised study/lessons)

2. **Is Alex's course funded by an employer, or has Alex agreed to work for an employer after the course?**

For most sixth form students doing A-levels, the answer would typically be "yes" to full-time and "no" to employer-funded. Does this sound right for Alex's situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. Alex is doing full-time A-levels - he's at sixth form college during the week with all his classes and coursework. And no, it's not funded by any employer or anything like that - it's just regular sixth form education.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, that all sounds good for Alex's education!

- ✓ A-levels are an approved (non-advanced) qualification
- ✓ Full-time study (12+ hours per week)
- ✓ Not employer-funded

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about Alex's work situation.

**Does Alex work less than 24 hours per week in paid employment?** (Or perhaps he doesn't have a paid job at all?)

This includes any part-time jobs he might have alongside his studies.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't have a job at all. He's just focused on his studies at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great - no job means this requirement is easily met.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thanks! Next question:

**Is Alex NOT claiming certain benefits in his own right?**

Specifically, is Alex free from claiming:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

(Most young people in full-time education wouldn't be claiming these, but I need to check.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't claim any benefits in his own name. He's just a student living at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's another box ticked!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Just to confirm:

**Is Alex doing an apprenticeship, or is he purely in sixth form education (A-levels)?**

You've mentioned he's doing A-levels at sixth form, so I'm assuming he's not on any kind of apprenticeship programme - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - he's just doing A-levels at sixth form, not an apprenticeship or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that's confirmed - Alex is in full-time A-level education, not an apprenticeship.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now we need to check your residency status. 

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You only need to meet **ONE** of these:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (most common)
2. You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have **pre-settled status** AND are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have **pre-settled status** AND are actively seeking work
5. You have **pre-settled status** AND have sufficient financial resources
6. You have **pre-settled status** AND are a student with sufficient resources
7. You have **pre-settled status** AND are a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You're a **Crown servant** living abroad

Do any of these apply to you? For most people living in the UK, option 1 (normally living in the UK) would apply.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I normally live in the UK - I live in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, living in Manchester means you meet the UK residency requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check that you're responsible for Alex. 

**Do any of the following apply to you?**

You only need to meet **ONE** of these:

1. **You live with Alex** (most common)
2. **You contribute to Alex's upkeep** (e.g., paying for food, clothes, housing, pocket money) - at least the same amount as Child Benefit
3. **You pay maintenance** for Alex under a court order or agreement

You mentioned Alex lives at home with you, so I assume option 1 applies - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good, that's confirmed. Now an important question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- Another family member

This could be:
1. **No one else is claiming** for Alex
2. **Someone else was claiming, but they've agreed to transfer** the claim to you
3. **There's a dispute** about who should claim

Which applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that keeps things straightforward.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check Alex's living situation a bit more specifically.

**Where is Alex currently living?**

1. **Normal living** - Living at home with you (or away at school/college but comes home regularly)
2. **In hospital** - Currently staying in hospital
3. **In local authority care** - Being looked after by the local council
4. **In foster care** - Being fostered

Since you mentioned Alex lives at home with you, I assume it's option 1 (normal living) - is that right?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex just lives at home with me normally. He goes to sixth form college during the day and comes home in the evenings.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's a normal living situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
We're almost there! One final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, you're supporting Alex yourself without financial assistance from the council for his care or housing. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct - the council isn't paying anything towards Alex. I'm supporting him myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! That's all the questions I need to ask.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Great news! You ARE ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit for Alex!

### Summary

You meet all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit:

**For Alex:**
- ✓ Age 17 (within eligible 16-19 range)
- ✓ In approved full-time education (A-levels at sixth form)
- ✓ Not working 24+ hours per week
- ✓ Not claiming benefits in his own right
- ✓ Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship

**For you (the claimant):**
- ✓ Normally resident in the UK
- ✓ Responsible for Alex (living with him)
- ✓ No other person claiming for Alex
- ✓ Council not paying for Alex's care

---

### Next Steps to Claim

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** (or adoption certificate if applicable)
4. **Payments** are usually made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### Important Note
⚠️ **High Income Child Benefit Charge**: If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay back some or all of the Child Benefit through a tax charge. You can still claim, but it's worth checking if this applies to you.

👉 **Apply here**: [https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old (within the 16-19 age bracket). He is in approved non-advanced education (A-levels at sixth form), studying full-time (12+ hours per week), and the course is not employer-funded. Alex does not work, does not claim benefits in his own right (ESA or Universal Credit), and is not on an apprenticeship. The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester), lives with Alex and is responsible for him, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their son Alex. Alex qualifies as a dependent child because he is 17, in full-time approved education (A-levels), not working, and not claiming benefits. The claimant meets all residency and responsibility requirements.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old (within the 16-19 age bracket). He is in approved non-advanced education (A-levels at sixth form), studying full-time (12+ hours per week), and the course is not employer-funded. Alex does not work, does not claim benefits in his own right (ESA or Universal Credit), and is not on an apprenticeship. The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester), lives with Alex and is responsible for him, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their son Alex. Alex qualifies as a dependent child because he is 17, in full-time approved education (A-levels), not working, and not claiming benefits. The claimant meets all residency and responsibility requirements.
